title: Humility Is for Everyone
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Humility is for Everyone - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 Peter 5_1-7.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Humility Is the Year's Theme — and It Is for Everyone
subpoints: This is the fourth and concluding sermon in the 2022 humility series; the theme for the year is growing in humility
Humility: dying more and more to self and sin, living more and more for others and unto righteousness; living Galatians 2:20

The word for humility in the first-century Greco-Roman world was a pejorative — why would anyone voluntarily lower himself for others? Yet the Holy Spirit used exactly that word for our Savior and for us
1 Peter 5:1-7 shows that humility is required of everyone: elders (vv.1-4), younger people (v.5a), and the whole church (v.5b-7)

outline.title: 1. Elders Are to Be Humble — Serving, Not Lording Over
subpoints: Verse 2: elders are to shepherd willingly, not by compulsion; not for dishonest gain but eagerly
Verse 3: not lording it over those entrusted to them but being examples to the flock
Verse 4: they hold office not for present glory but looking to the crown that will not fade at the coming of the Chief Shepherd
Humility in leadership means executing the office for the good of others, not for personal gain or power

outline.title: 2. Younger People Are to Submit to Elders
subpoints: Verse 5a: 'Likewise you younger people, submit yourselves to your elders' — the word 'elders' is the same word as in verse 1, referring to church officers, not merely older people
The word for 'younger people' is masculine plural — may refer specifically to younger men or to the whole group; either way, submission to church authority is the application
Wayne Grudem: if those most likely to be independent-minded (younger men) are commanded to be subject to elders, then certainly everyone else must be as well
Submitting to church authority is not unique to the young: Titus 3:1-2 and Hebrews 13:17 address the whole congregation

outline.title: 3. Everyone Is to Be Submissive to One Another
subpoints: Verse 5b: 'Yes, all of you be submissive to one another and be clothed with humility' — mutual submission is a sign of the Spirit's filling (Ephesians 5:21)
Peter uses a unique word for 'clothed' that refers to a slave putting on his apron to serve — perhaps recalling Jesus washing the disciples' feet in the upper room
We were made to live interdependently; only the proud thinks he needs no one; the American spirit of independence is not a Christian virtue when it leads to isolation
Romans 12:10: 'Be kindly affectionate to one another with brotherly love, giving preference to one another' — that is mutual submission

outline.title: 4. How Humility Is Attained — Growing in It
subpoints: Mutual submission (v.5): acknowledges we need each other; breaks down the proud thought that we have conquered all our sins; without it we miss our own blind spots (Hebrews 3:12-13)
Being clothed in humility as a slave ready to serve — taking the lowest tasks for the good of others, not condescendingly but as a doulos

Hurling ourselves upon God (vv.6-7): the word 'casting' in verse 7 is used only twice in the New Testament; it means to hurl or throw; it is a decisive act — not wading gradually into a pool but jumping into the deep end all at once
Anxiety is more often than not a sign of pride — thinking that God may get tomorrow wrong, and that our worrying can fix it; humility casts all cares on God who cares for us

outline.title: Conclusion: The Warning and the Reward
subpoints: Verse 5: 'God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble' — the word 'resists' means to oppose, to go to war against; God is hostile to those who refuse to humble themselves
The proud is the arrogant one who trusts in himself and will not place himself under authority — really the disposition of an unbeliever; a Christian who is proud is acting like an unbeliever
The reward of humility: grace to grow in Christ (v.5); exaltation in due time (v.6) — as God exalted his Son after his humiliation; God will raise us from the dead and we will be with Christ forever
Heidelberg Catechism Q.26: I trust God so much that I do not doubt he will provide whatever I need for body and soul and will turn to my good whatever adversity he sends

summary:
This fourth and final sermon in the 2022 humility series examines 1 Peter 5:1-7, arguing that humility is not the calling of a particular class within the church — it is the calling of everyone without exception. Pastor Tito Lyro works through each group Peter addresses: elders are to shepherd willingly, not for gain, not lording it over but serving as examples; younger people are to submit to the authority of the elders; and the whole congregation is to be clothed with humility toward one another. The word Peter uses for 'clothed' uniquely refers to a slave putting on a work apron — perhaps recalling Christ's own towel-wrapped service at the Last Supper.

The sermon then addresses how humility is grown: through mutual submission that exposes our blind spots, through slave-like service of others, and above all through hurling ourselves upon God. The word 'casting' in verse 7 appears only twice in the New Testament and means to throw or hurl — it is a decisive act, not a gradual process. Anxiety, Pastor Lyro argues, is usually a form of pride — the proud thought that God may get tomorrow wrong and our worrying can fix it. The humble cast their cares on God because he cares for them.

The sermon closes with the double edge of the passage's warning and promise: God is literally at war with the proud, but gives grace — his infinite, marvelous, transforming grace — to the humble. And the humble will be exalted in due time, as God exalted his Son who humbled himself to the point of death on a cross.
transcript:
## Introduction: A Year of Humility

Returning to 1 Peter chapter 5, I am going to read verses 1 through 7:

*The elders who are among you I exhort, I who am a fellow elder and a witness of the sufferings of Christ, and also a partaker of the glory that will be revealed: shepherd the flock of God which is among you, serving as overseers, not by compulsion, but willingly, not for dishonest gain, but eagerly; nor as being lords over those entrusted to you, but being examples to the flock. And when the Chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the crown of glory that does not fade away. Likewise, you younger people, submit yourselves to your elders. Yes, all of you be submissive to one another, and be clothed with humility, for God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble. Therefore humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, that He may exalt you in due time, casting all your care upon Him, for He cares for you.*

Our Lord Jesus Christ describes himself as gentle and lowly. His ministry is described as lowering or humbling himself for others. It is clear in the Bible that humility is a quality in which God delights — and equally clear that God desires his people to be humble in his sight.

It is interesting that when the New Testament scriptures were written, the Holy Spirit chose to use a word for humility — for meekness, for lowliness — that in the secular world was a cussword. To be humble in the first century was to be someone who did not deserve any attention. Why would someone desire to lower himself for the benefit of others? To be called humble was to be insulted. And yet the Holy Spirit inspired the apostles to use exactly that word — the word derided by the world — to describe not only us, but our Savior himself, who was derided by the world in every aspect.

We started 2022 with four sermons on humility, with the purpose of setting this year apart as the year in which we all grow in humility. In those four sermons we have considered that humility is dying more and more to self and to sin, and living more and more for others and unto righteousness. Humility is living Galatians 2:20: 'I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ lives in me. And the life which I now live in the flesh I live by faith in the Son of God who loved me and gave himself for me.' Humility is embodied day to day as we live according to the John the Baptist principle: Christ must increase; I must decrease. Humility is losing ourselves for the sake of the gospel.

It is hard to believe that today marks the end of the first quarter of 2022. It seems like just yesterday we were at Isaac and Kenzie's wedding celebrating their reunion — and now it is three months later. And so I thought it would be good to remind ourselves of the high calling that God has in our lives to be a humble people — especially by reminding ourselves that humility is for everyone. We might even think: yes, humility is great; so-and-so really needs to be humble. Yet the Scripture says that is the calling of all of us.

## 1. Elders Are to Be Humble

In verses 1-4, we see that the leaders of the church are to be humble. Look at how Peter describes the way they are to perform their duties as elders. In verse 2, it is described as serving — something they do willingly, not for dishonest gain. These are all aspects of humility in the office of elder. In verse 3, they do not lord it over the people they are shepherding; they lead by being examples. In verse 4, they hold the office of an elder not for present glory — because they are looking for the reward, the glory that will be given to them at the resurrection, when the Lord Jesus Christ comes back.

## 2. Younger People Are to Submit to Elders

In verse 5, Peter says: 'Likewise, you younger people, submit yourselves to your elders.' A couple of things need to be said. First, the word translated 'elders' in this verse is the exact same word as in verse 1 — where Peter says, 'The elders who are among you, I exhort.' In verse 1, it clearly refers to those who hold a particular office in the church — not just old people. It is very unlikely that Peter would use the same word in the same context to mean something entirely different four verses later.

The word 'younger people' is masculine. In Greek, masculine plural words often refer to the totality of a group regardless of gender — so 'younger people' likely addresses all the younger members of the church who may be naturally more independent-minded and less inclined to submit to leadership.

Wayne Grudem, the well-known theologian and New Testament scholar who wrote a commentary on 1 Peter, thinks this addresses the younger men in the church — those most likely to be independent-minded, even rebellious against church leaders. His point is: if those who have the hardest time doing this are commanded to be subject to the elders, certainly everyone else must be as well.

One way humility in younger people manifests itself is in submission to the elders of the church. But I want to be clear: submission to church authority is not unique to the younger people. Paul in Titus 3:1-2 is instructing a pastor overseeing several churches, and he tells Titus: 'Remind them to be subject to rulers and authorities, to obey, to be ready for every good work, to speak evil of no one, to be peaceful, gentle, showing all humility to all men.' The whole church is addressed. In Hebrews 13:17, referring to the whole church: 'Obey those who rule over you, and be submissive, for they watch out for your souls as those who must give account.' It will be unprofitable for you if you do not.

## 3. Everyone Is to Be Submissive to One Another

Peter does not stop with younger people. Look at the second half of verse 5: 'Yes, all of you be submissive to one another and be clothed with humility.' Peter uses the exact same word — submission — to describe our relationship to one another. Everyone has to be submissive. Humility includes submission to one another, which includes dependence on one another.

The American spirit of independence may lead us to believe that independence is always a virtue — but that is not the case. We were made to live interdependently. Only the proud does not recognize that he or she needs others. Paul describes a Christian who is filled with the Spirit in Ephesians 5:21 as 'one who is submitting to one another in the fear of God.' A sign that you are filled with the Spirit is that you are willing to submit to one another. Humble brotherly love is partly described as mutual submission — Romans 12:10: 'Be kindly affectionate to one another with brotherly love, giving preference to one another.'

It is interesting that Peter uses a unique word for 'clothed' here. Paul likes the idea of putting off and putting on garments — but he uses the general word for taking clothes off and putting them on. Peter uses a different word: one that refers not to just anyone putting on clothing, but to a slave putting on his or her apron to get ready to serve others. To be clothed in humility means that you are preparing yourself to serve others regardless of how menial the task may be. Perhaps Peter has in mind that scene in the upper room where Jesus himself dressed as a slave and served his disciples by washing their feet. Clothing yourself with humility means you are preparing yourself to serve as a slave, not as a condescending person, but as a doulos who comes to serve a master.

## 4. How Is Humility Attained?

Peter is writing to those who have been redeemed by the blood of Christ — people born again to a living hope. When we talk about attaining humility, we are not pulling ourselves up by our own bootstraps. We are relying on the grace of God and the indwelling of the Spirit of Christ to grow in righteousness. At the same time, that relying on God's grace looks like real work on our part.

First, we grow in humility through mutual submission (v.5). Submitting to one another acknowledges that we need each other, that we are not self-sufficient. It breaks down the proud thought that we have conquered all our sins — or the proud thought that we must hide our sins so people won't find out. I guarantee there is no one here who thinks you don't sin. The cat is long out of the bag. So we do not need to pretend. The author of Hebrews tells us in Hebrews 3:12-13 that there are sin blind spots in our lives, and if we do not allow people in, we are going to miss what we cannot see. 'Exhort one another daily while it is called today, lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin.'

Second, we grow in humility by being clothed in it — putting on the slave's apron and preparing to serve others, whatever the task.

Third — and this is the key — we grow in humility by throwing ourselves upon the Lord. Look at verses 6 and 7: 'Therefore humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, that He may exalt you in due time, casting all your care upon Him for He cares for you.' The word 'casting' in verse 7 describes the act of humbling ourselves in verse 6. Verse 6 says: humble yourself — how? By casting yourself upon the Lord.

This word is used only twice in the New Testament. In Luke 19:35 it describes the disciples throwing their cloaks upon the donkey that Jesus rode into Jerusalem. A Greek lexicon defines it as 'to throw or hurl something upon something else.' So get the picture: Peter says hurl yourself upon Christ. And it is a decisive action — not a process. It is not like wading into a pool, getting your toes in first and then gradually going deeper. It is like jumping into the deep end right away.

Anxiety more often than not reflects a heart that thinks it can control tomorrow. Anxiety is thinking that God may get tomorrow wrong and that, if I really worry about it, I can make it right — because I cannot trust God to handle it. Is that the humble position? No, that is the proud position. The proud position thinks that even though God might get it wrong, I can get it right if I worry enough. Peter says: hurl all your anxieties upon him. And the reason we can is that he cares for you.

The Heidelberg Catechism, question 26, asks what it means to believe that God is the Father Almighty. The answer includes: 'I trust God so much that I do not doubt he will provide whatever I need for body and soul and will turn to my good whatever adversity he sends upon me in this sad world. God is able to do this because he is Almighty God and desires to do this because he is a faithful Father.' That is why we can trust him.

## Conclusion: Warning and Reward

There is a great warning in verse 5 concerning the lack of humility: 'God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble.' Peter here quotes Proverbs 3:34. The English word 'resists' does not do full justice to the word Peter uses. It literally means to oppose someone — to go to battle against someone, including hostility toward someone. Peter is saying that God is hostile and wages war against those who refuse to humble themselves under his mighty hand. The proud — the arrogant, the one who trusts himself and will not place himself under authority — is really describing the disposition of the unbeliever. A Christian who is proud is thinking and behaving like an unbeliever. And God is at war with that. God never loses a battle.

But there is a great reward in humility. 'God gives grace to the humble' — his infinite, marvelous grace, that causes us to grow in Jesus Christ, that enables us to die more and more to ourselves, that brings us closer to God. Isaiah 57:15: 'The high and lofty One who inhabits eternity, whose name is holy — I dwell in the high and holy place with him who has a contrite and humble spirit, to revive the spirit of the humble and to revive the heart of the contrite ones.' The greatest being in existence enjoys the company of the humble.

And there is the reward of exaltation. Verse 6: 'Humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God, that He may exalt you in due time.' God lifts up those who lower themselves before him. He did that to Jesus — Philippians 2:9: 'Therefore God highly exalted him and given him the name that is above every name.' And he will do that for us as well. He exalts us even in this life — 'Trust in the Lord with all your heart... and he will make your paths smooth.' And certainly it is true in the life to come, where we will be glorified in him.

As we enter the second quarter of 2022, let us humble ourselves under the mighty hand of God, knowing that he cares for us as demonstrated in his giving of his Son for us. Let us cast aside pride and cast ourselves upon the God who exalts the humble. And we do that by turning our eyes upon Jesus.

Father, thank you for your Word. We pray that the words proclaimed this morning from this text would penetrate deeply into all of our hearts, that we might grow in humility. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
